entries.title: Telemetry Distribution Context
entries.content:
Error: include
-----
Cannot call function include(MutableContext context, String path, optional ContextSandbox sandbox) with arguments (./_setup.qd): 
File c:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\.\_setup.qd does not exist.

	.include {.pathtoroot/_setup.qd}

Telemetry Distribution Context

Realised by: contracts-service · Subdomain type: Core

This page describes the design-time model of the Telemetry Distribution context. The runtime mechanics — how filtering is made fast, how streams are carried to the browser — are architectural concerns documented in the Error
-----
Cannot access file c:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\..\services\contracts.qd: not enough privileges to perform this action. 
Missing required permission: global-read. 
To grant this permission, run with: --allow global-read

 and Error
-----
Cannot access file c:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\..\architecture\overview.qd: not enough privileges to perform this action. 
Missing required permission: global-read. 
To grant this permission, run with: --allow global-read

 pages.

At the domain level, this context decides which metrics each building’s live view should carry, and shapes the flow of readings accordingly.

-----

Ubiquitous Language

PreferenceAllowed metricMetric CatalogDistributionTermThe ordered set of metrics a Building has chosen to display.A single metric included in a Preference.The combined list of metrics offered by the upstream contexts.Forwarding a reading only to the Buildings whose Preference includes its metric.Meaning in this context

-----

The Building Preference Aggregate

The aggregate root is a Building’s Preference: which metrics it shows, in what order. Order is part of the model, because it is the order the display is meant to present.



- A Preference is keyed by its Building, so there is exactly one Preference per Building.
- The allowed metrics are an ordered collection; reordering is a meaningful change to the model, not an incidental detail.
- We left open, at design time, how durable a Preference must be and how quickly a change must take effect — those service-level qualities are decided in the implementation.

-----

The Metric Catalog

This context does not invent metrics — it assembles them. The design intent is that each upstream context describes its own metrics (see the Published Language in Telemetry Ingestion and the structural metrics owned by Building Management, and this context gathers those descriptions into one catalog the display can choose from.



Gathering rather than defining keeps this context a Conformist consumer of each upstream Published Language: a new metric upstream becomes selectable here without a coordinated change.

-----

Distribution as a Domain Rule

The central rule of this context is simple to state at the domain level: a reading should reach a Building’s live view only if that Building’s Preference includes the reading’s metric.

	distribute(reading):
	    for each building preference:
	        if reading.metric in preference.allowedMetrics:
	            deliver the reading to that building's view

The model says nothing about how readings arrive or are carried onward — that the rule must hold at high throughput is a quality we hand to the implementation.

-----

A note on transport

Carrying readings to the browser is, by design, not part of this context’s model. We classify real-time transport as a generic subdomain (see Strategic Design: it holds no aggregates and enforces no rules, so it is kept out of the domain model and treated as infrastructure.

entries.title: Telemetry Ingestion Context
entries.content:
Error: include
-----
Cannot call function include(MutableContext context, String path, optional ContextSandbox sandbox) with arguments (./_setup.qd): 
File C:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\.\_setup.qd does not exist.

	.include {.pathtoroot/_setup.qd}

Telemetry Ingestion Context

Realised by: sensor-service · Subdomain type: Core

This page describes the design-time model of the Telemetry Ingestion context. How the service is structured to accept and route readings at runtime — the registry/dispatch mechanism that lets new sensor types be added — is an architectural concern and is covered in Error
-----
Cannot access file C:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\..\architecture\microservices.qd: not enough privileges to perform this action. 
Missing required permission: global-read. 
To grant this permission, run with: --allow global-read

 and the Error
-----
Cannot access file C:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\..\services\sensor.qd: not enough privileges to perform this action. 
Missing required permission: global-read. 
To grant this permission, run with: --allow global-read

 page, not here.

At the domain level, this context turns raw sensor input into validated, time-stamped readings, and holds the thresholds those readings are judged against.

-----

Ubiquitous Language

SignalMetricThresholdReading windowTermA single time-stamped reading from one room (people count, temperature, air quality).A category of signal, identified by a stable key.A bound a metric is expected to stay within, for a building or a room.The time range over which signals are aggregated for display.Meaning in this context

-----

Signals as Aggregates

We treat each metric as a small aggregate root of its own: an append-only reading that shares a common identity (which building, which room, when) plus its metric-specific values. Signals are conceptually immutable — a correction is a new reading, not an edit of an old one.



We chose one aggregate per metric rather than a single generic “reading” so that each metric can carry its own fields and grow its own rules without entangling the others. Whether the set of metrics is fixed or open-ended was left flexible at this stage — the model is meant to accommodate metrics we have not designed yet.

-----

The Threshold Aggregate

BuildingThreshold is the one mutable aggregate in this context: the consistency boundary around all of a Building’s configured bounds, with optional per-room overrides modelled as value objects.



- We keep at most one threshold configuration per Building, so the aggregate owns the complete picture of that Building’s bounds.
- Intended rule: a room-level bound, when present, takes precedence over the building-level default for that room. Resolving the effective bound for a given reading is domain logic.

-----

Validation as a Domain Rule

Accepting a reading is a domain decision, not just a transport check. The intent is that an incoming reading is judged against the model before it is accepted, and that the outcome is a structured verdict the caller can act on — not an exception.

	validate(reading):
	    confirm the reading identifies a building, a room, and a time
	    confirm the metric-specific values are present and well-formed
	    return ok, or the list of reasons it was rejected

Designing validation to return a verdict (rather than throw) is a deliberate choice so that a rejected reading can be reported clearly to its source.



-----

Published Language: the Metric description

For the Telemetry Distribution context to make sense of readings without bespoke coupling, this context is meant to describe its own metrics — their key, label, unit, and fields — as a small, stable contract.

	Metric:
	    key         -- stable identifier, e.g. "temperature"
	    label       -- human-readable name
	    unit        -- optional unit of measure
	    fields[]    -- the shape of a reading of this metric

Publishing a self-describing metric is what allows a new metric type to become visible downstream without a coordinated change — an application of the Open Host Service / Published Language idea introduced in Strategic Design.

entries.title: Strategic Design
entries.content:
Error: include
-----
Cannot call function include(MutableContext context, String path, optional ContextSandbox sandbox) with arguments (./_setup.qd): 
File C:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\.\_setup.qd does not exist.

	.include {.pathtoroot/_setup.qd}

Strategic Design

CrowdVision is modelled with a Domain-Driven Design (DDD) mindset. We approached the system not as one monolithic model but as a set of collaborating models, each owned by a context and each speaking its own dialect of a shared Ubiquitous Language. This page captures the strategic design decisions taken up front — the subdomains, the bounded contexts, and the relationships between them. The tactical model (aggregates, entities, value objects, domain services) is described per context on the following pages.

A pragmatic stance
-----
We treat DDD as a design compass, not a rulebook. The strategic boundaries below are the part of the design we were most confident in. The tactical model is applied where it earns its keep, and some boundaries were left intentionally soft because the product ideas were still maturing when this design was written. The per-context pages flag where a decision is provisional or expected to evolve.

-----

Domain and Subdomains

The overall domain is real-time monitoring of physical spaces through a digital twin. We classify the subdomains by how much competitive value they carry, which in turn drives how much modelling effort each is given.

Spatial Digital TwinTelemetry Ingestion & ThresholdsTelemetry DistributionIdentity & AccessAlertingKnowledge & AssistanceReal-Time TransportSubdomainCoreCoreCoreSupportingSupportingSupportingGenericTypetwin-servicesensor-servicecontracts-serviceauth-servicenotification-serviceagent-servicesocket-serviceRealised byThe 3D building model is the product’s differentiator and receives the richest model.Turning raw sensor signals into meaningful, bounded readings is central business logic.Per-building stream shaping is what makes the live view feel tailored.Multi-tenant access control is essential but not a differentiator.Push alerting supports the core experience without defining it.The assistant augments the product; it is explicitly experimental.A commodity relay with no domain model of its own.Rationale

-----

Bounded Contexts

A guiding decision is that each core and supporting subdomain becomes exactly one bounded context, and each context is meant to stand on its own — its own model, its own storage, its own boundary. We chose this alignment so that a model can never quietly leak into another context through shared data: a context boundary is also intended to be a deployment and storage boundary.



The Real-Time Transport context is omitted from the map: it is meant to carry messages between contexts but to hold no model and enforce no rules.

-----

Context Map

We describe the relationships between contexts with the standard DDD integration patterns. They tell a contributor who depends on whom and which side dictates the contract. The interactions are stated conceptually; concrete endpoints belong to the implementation.

Identity → all other contextsBuilding Management → Telemetry IngestionBuilding Management → Telemetry DistributionTelemetry Ingestion → Telemetry DistributionBuilding Management → AlertingBuilding Management → Knowledge & AssistanceUpstream → DownstreamPublished LanguageCustomer / SupplierCustomer / SupplierOpen Host Service + Published LanguageCustomer / Supplier (Anti-Corruption)Anti-Corruption LayerPatternThe signed identity token (memberships, roles, domains) is the published language every context conforms to. Downstream contexts are Conformists: they read the token but never reshape it.When a Building is created, Building Management supplies a threshold baseline. It is the customer driving the contract.Building Management supplies default display preferences and contributes the structural metrics it owns to the catalog.Telemetry Ingestion describes its metrics in a stable, self-describing form. Distribution gathers those descriptions, so new metrics appear downstream without a coordinated change.Alerting asks Building Management which domains a building belongs to, and translates the answer into its own terms.The assistant reaches live building data only through a thin read-only layer, keeping the Building model out of the assistant’s domain.Integration

-----

The Shared Identifier Convention

Across the platform, we decided that a Domain is referenced by its name, never by an internal database identifier. This is the closest thing the design has to a Shared Kernel, and it is deliberately minimal: a single, stable, human-readable string rather than shared code.



Because the domain name travels inside the identity token and is recorded by each context, the intent is that multi-tenant isolation can be enforced symmetrically — each context answers “may this caller see this data?” from data it already holds, without consulting another context.

-----

Tactical Patterns at a Glance

The building blocks below recur throughout the per-context pages. The table states what each means in our model — not how any one service ends up implementing it.

Aggregate RootEntityValue ObjectDomain ServiceDomain EventDDD Building BlockA top-level concept that owns a consistency boundary, e.g. Account, Building, BuildingThreshold.Something with identity that lives inside an aggregate, e.g. Room within Building.An immutable, identity-less descriptor, e.g. Coordinates, Dimensions, a per-type preference.Behaviour that does not belong to a single entity, e.g. issuing the identity token, or deciding whether a reading is acceptable.Something noteworthy that happened, broadcast for other contexts to react to, e.g. a new reading or an alert.How it appears in our model

Where the model is deliberately light
-----
Some aggregates are intentionally thin at this stage — they describe data and a few rules rather than rich behaviour. This was a conscious trade-off while the contexts were still settling, and the per-context pages note where we expect behaviour to grow onto the aggregate as the design firms up.
